vitals.date_specific: Friday, May 21, 2027, 09:00 start; extends into the weekend under a 50-hour main-route time limit
vitals.field_size: Not documented in current sources — this is a small, self-selecting semi-autonomous ultra-distance field, not a mass-participation event
vitals.entry_fee: EUR240 solo (650km); duo and relay categories also offered
vitals.feed_zones: 3 official feed stations; personal assistance restricted to organizer life bases, no following vehicles permitted

climate.description: Late May across southwest-to-north-central France: variable spring weather over more than 24 hours of continuous riding, from Bordeaux's warmer south to cooler conditions approaching Paris.
challenges: Riding through a full day-night cycle exposes riders to a wide temperature swing and unpredictable spring weather across the route

terrain.surface: Paved roads, open to traffic — semi-autonomous ultra-distance format, not a closed-road race

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 60. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 56. SOL REVIEW FLAG (2026-07-24, not applied — numeric dimensions locked per wave-10 rule): reviewer notes climbing=1 badly understates a verified 4,500m route, and scenic_experience/organization/community_culture/value at 3-4 are not clearly evidenced by the research dump. Flagging for a human rescoring pass, not auto-applying — see also catalog_flags taxonomy note on whether this format belongs in the gran_fondo tier's rubric at all.

course_description.character: The modern Bordeaux-Paris is an amateur ultra-distance revival, not the motor-paced professional classic that ran 1891-1988 under the same name. It runs 650km from the Stadium Velodrome de Bordeaux to Domaine de Coubertin near Paris, semi-autonomous: riders navigate an unmarked route by GPX, carry mandatory safety and repair gear, and rely on three official feed stations and a mid-course rest area rather than continuous support. There's no competitive classification — only individual completion times inside a 50-hour limit. A shorter ~305km secondary route starts from Chateauroux for riders who want the format without the full distance.
course_description.signature_challenge: The 50-hour clock itself, across a full day-night cycle with minimal support — sleep management is treated as part of the event, not an afterthought, with organizer-provided mattresses at the mid-course rest area.
suffering_zones: The overnight hours on unmarked open roads, navigating by GPX alone between official life bases
The stretch between feed stations, where riders are self-sufficient and following vehicles are prohibited

logistics.lodging_strategy: Self-managed; pre/post-event hotels in Bordeaux and Paris.
logistics.food: Three official feed stations plus a mid-course rest area with mattresses on the 650km route; otherwise self-supported per the semi-autonomous rules.

history.origin_story: The historic professional Bordeaux-Paris classic ran 1891-1988 as a motor-paced monument. Extra Sports began developing a modern amateur revival in 2012 and staged its first modern edition, a 2014 mass-participation cyclosportive (~1,000 entrants, ~610km), with Golazo Sports and the Girondins de Bordeaux cycling organization. It returned in 2022 in its current ultra-distance, GPS-tracked, semi-autonomous format. The event is now owned and organized by Chablais Leman Sport Organisation ('CLSO by SMH'), a French FFC-affiliated nonprofit, with former professional Pierre Rolland involved in the revival — media coverage (L'Equipe) described him as the first finisher of the 2026 edition, though the event itself publishes no official competitive classification.
notable_moments: 2014: first modern cyclosportive revival with ~1,000 entrants under Extra Sports
2022: relaunch in the current ultra-distance/semi-autonomous format
2023 and 2024 editions were both cancelled before the event resumed
2026: Pierre Rolland, retired from professional racing since 2022, was reported by L'Equipe as the first finisher
history.reputation: A real historic name attached to a genuinely modern ultra-distance format — credible among amateur ultra-cycling riders, but with a rocky recent history of cancelled editions and site-level inconsistencies that riders should verify before entering.

biased_opinion.verdict: Real Ultra Revival, Not the Race Its Name Implies
biased_opinion.summary: The name Bordeaux-Paris carries a century of professional cycling history — but the event you'd actually sign up for today is a 650km semi-autonomous ultra-distance ride with no official competitive classification, revived by Extra Sports in 2014 and relaunched in its current GPS-tracked, no-classification format in 2022. It's a real, currently organized event (CLSO by SMH, an FFC-affiliated nonprofit) with a retired professional rider, Pierre Rolland, reported by L'Equipe as the 2026 edition's first finisher despite the event publishing no formal results ranking. But it's not a gran fondo in any conventional sense — there's no field to race against, no closed roads, minimal aid; it's a solo clock-and-navigation test with three feed stations and a mattress at the midpoint. The 2023 and 2024 editions were both cancelled, which the organizer itself has acknowledged left a bad taste for prospective entrants, and the current website mixes 2026 and 2027 documentation in ways worth double-checking before you pay.
strengths: A currently organized event with a French-federation-affiliated nonprofit operator and a retired professional (Pierre Rolland) among recent participants
Historic route concept (Bordeaux to Paris) with real cultural weight, even though the format has changed completely from the professional original
Multiple entry formats (solo, duo, two-person relay, four-person relay) for different team structures
Sleep and pacing built into the format rather than ignored — a mid-course rest area with mattresses
weaknesses: Does not fit standard gran-fondo dimensions — no competitive classification, no closed roads, minimal support; this is ultra-distance riding, not a fondo
Both the 2023 and 2024 editions were cancelled outright, and the organizer has publicly acknowledged the resulting reputational damage
Current official site has real inconsistencies (300 vs 310km secondary route, stale 2026 documents, a registration link still pointing to a '2026' URL path while selling 2027 entries)

A detailed 2022 finisher account describes conflicting GPS directions, an understocked feed station, and apparent support-rule violations — from the prior Extra Sports operation, not necessarily the current CLSO one, but worth knowing
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you want to ride a route with a real name and real history, this delivers that — but understand you're signing up for a solo semi-autonomous ultra-distance test with no official results ranking and a rocky recent track record, not a supported gran fondo.

final_verdict.one_liner: A historic name attached to a genuinely modern ultra-distance revival — with a rocky recent past.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Only if you specifically want a semi-autonomous, minimally-supported ultra-distance challenge — this is not a supported gran fondo, and you should verify current registration details directly given the site's documented inconsistencies and the 2023/2024 cancellations.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a supported, competitively classified road gran fondo instead, look elsewhere in this database's standard gran-fondo tier. For another historic-route French ultra-distance event, Paris-Brest-Paris is the better-known comparison — verify its own current format and classification directly rather than assuming parity with this event.

key_challenges.name: Opening 500 meters
key_challenges.description: Riders in 1903 complained about cart tracks and terrible road conditions for the first 500 meters, causing the start to be delayed.

terrain_notes.text: The original route included dirt roads that would have made maintaining racing speed extremely difficult compared to modern perfect tarmac.

gear_mentions.text: Historical bikes featured gearing around 45-46 front and 18 rear, with unreliable back brakes that gave riders no confidence on descents.

race_day_tips.text: The original 1903 stage started at 16 minutes past three in the afternoon, suggesting strategic timing considerations for the long distance.

additional_quotes.text: The bearings are also quite old so it seems as though the bottom bracket is kind of seized which makes it slightly difficult to pedal

rider_intel.search_text: This historical recreation of the original 1903 Tour de France first stage from Paris to Lyon covered 467 kilometers, double the distance of modern Tour stages. Riders describe the challenge as utterly indescribably tough and one of the most ridiculous things ever done on two wheels. The original route featured terrible road conditions including cart tracks and dirt surfaces that made maintaining racing pace extremely difficult. Historical equipment included heavy bikes with unreliable back brakes, aggressive racing positions despite the ultra-distance, and limited gearing around 45-46 front chainring with 18-tooth rear. The 1903 stage started at 3:16 PM after delays due to rider complaints about poor road conditions. Only two riders finished the stage together in a breakaway, including winner Maurice Garan and an amateur underdog, highlighting the extreme difficulty and attrition rate of early Tour stages.

eligibility.notes: Real, currently organized amateur ultra-cycling revival of the historic 1891-1988 professional classic — not fabricated, not a continuation of the original professional format. Next edition announced for May 21, 2027 following a completed 2026 event with results. Both the 2023 and 2024 editions were cancelled before the event resumed in 2025/2026 under current organizer CLSO by SMH. Current site mixes 2026 and 2027 documentation (registration link still shows a '2026' URL path); verify current-year details directly before entering.

catalog_flags.taxonomy_note: This is a semi-autonomous/semi-supported ultra-distance event (650km, no competitive classification, minimal aid) revived from the historic 1891-1988 professional Bordeaux-Paris classic — not a standard supported gran fondo. The fondo_rating dimensions (field_depth, prestige, organization, road_surface, etc.) were designed for supported gran fondos and map poorly onto this format. Flagging for a human call on whether it belongs in this database's gran_fondo tier at all, per the flandrien-ride precedent; discipline enum left unchanged pending that decision.
